history.label: Origins
history.text: The first town formation known in North Norway, Vågar, was situated around the narrow, natural harbor near Kabelvåg, just west of Svolvær.
Initially, the new municipality had 2,429 residents and it included 7.3 square kilometres (2.8 sq mi) of land.

The arms were designed by John Johnsen. Municipal self-government 1918-1963 Svolvær's historical significance as an important fishing village allowed the town to be granted town status (ladested) on 1 July 1918.

history.label: 1960s
history.text: During the 1960s, there were many municipal mergers across Norway due to the work of the Schei Committee.
Due to this merger in 1964, Svolvær lost its status as a "town" (ladested).
Following new legislation in Norway in 1996, the municipal council of Vågan Municipality voted to make Svolvær a "town" once again.

history.label: Growth
history.text: Vågar is mentioned in the book Heimskringla, and might have been established as early as the year 800 AD.

history.label: Modern city
history.text: Atlantic Cod fisheries, particularly during winter months, have remained one of the most important economical foundations for the town.

history.label: Today
history.text: Other industries which have proved to be valuable resources for Vågan are fish farming (salmon), Secora and Lofotkraft. Name The town (and former municipality) is named after the old Svolvær farm (Old Norse: Svǫlver).

history.label: Regional role
history.text: The official blazon is "Azure, a cod naiant argent in between a bar gemel wavy" (Norwegian: På blå bunn en vannrett sølv torsk mellom to bølgede strenger av sølv).

history.label: Regional role
history.text: The blue color and the design of the arms symbolize the importance of the sea and fishing for the municipality.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: The municipality is governed by a municipal council of directly elected representatives.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: The mayor is indirectly elected by a vote of the municipal council.
